---
title: "Setup"
author: "Paul Rougieux"
date: "2025"
output: 
  html_document: 
    toc: yes
---

This is a description of how I edit text files for persons looking at my screen 
and wondering what editor I use to move so fast. Does a text editor still 
matter in the age of AI?

Let me describe my text editing setup. This text was written by a human with 
his fingers on a keyboard in Vim.


# Text editing

I use Vim or Neovim to edit markdown files and source code. Mostly python code 
as of 2025, occasionally R code or other programming languages as well.

When editing python code, the most used shortcut is `<leaderkey>p` which sends 
a paragraph to the ipython read–eval–print loop (REPL). This shortcut uses 
[vim-slime](https://github.com/jpalardy/vim-slime) to "grab some text and send 
it to a target" tmux pane.


# Server setup


## Connect with alias in ssh config

See config in ~/.ssh/config
The password can be saved in 


## Create users

**On the server**

Log in as root and run these:

Create user: 

    adduser modeluser

Grant sudo: 

    usermod -aG sudo modeluser

Set password: 

    passwd modeluser.

Disable root SSH: Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config, set PermitRootLogin no, then systemctl restart ssh.

**On the laptop**

Update your ~/.ssh/config alias to use User modeluser.

Copy the public key in remote server as authorized keys
First check which public key is the most recent.

    scp $HOME/.ssh/id_rsa.pub user@server:~/.ssh/authorized_keys

**On the server**

- disable password authentication

  vi /etc/ssh/sshd_config

    PasswordAuthentication no
    ChallengeResponseAuthentication no
    PubkeyAuthentication yes


## Use authentication tokens to clone private repos
